claim: Context engineering is the art and science of filling the context window with just the right information at each step of an agent's trajectory
actionable: When building agents, systematically evaluate what context information is necessary at each step, removing irrelevant or redundant information to prevent token bloat and distraction

claim: Larger system prompts lead to sensory overload in frontier models—GPT-5.3's prompt is 1/3 the size of GPT-5's because frontier models perform better with fewer instructions
actionable: Progressively reduce system prompt size when upgrading models; test empirically that added instructions don't degrade agent performance

claim: Agents degrade after ~30 tools and completely fail by 100 tools; use RAG over tool descriptions with embedding-based semantic similarity retrieval to select relevant tools dynamically
actionable: For agents with >30 tools, implement embedding-based tool retrieval rather than passing all tool definitions in context; re-rank semantically similar tools based on task

claim: Four core strategies for context engineering: writing (scratch pads, memory), selecting (retrieving relevant context), compressing (summarization, trimming), and isolating (sandboxing, state partitioning, multi-agent)
actionable: Classify your context management challenges into these four buckets, then apply the appropriate technique for each

claim: LangGraph's state object supports context isolation by partitioning information into separate fields; expose only necessary fields to the LLM at each step
actionable: Design your LangGraph state with multiple fields (messages, scratchpad, long_term_memory, etc.) and selectively expose fields to the LLM based on the current node's needs

claim: Multi-agent systems expand total token processing capacity by distributing context across independent agents with their own context windows
actionable: For high-complexity tasks, decompose into parallel sub-agents each researching independent aspects, then synthesize results
